title: The worlds that Islam made
gist: Reviewing James McDougall's 'Worlds of Islam' and Fitzroy Morrissey's 'The Renewal of Islam,' Shiraz Maher argues Islam's global spread was driven mainly by trade and pragmatic adaptability rather than coercion, and that its lack of a centralised clergy makes it a uniquely 'democratised,' contested faith — a structural feature visible today in both radicalisation pipelines (Anjem Choudary) and Mohammed bin Salman's public-interest-based reinterpretation of Wahhabi doctrine to justify Saudi liberalisation.

insights.claim: James McDougall's 'Worlds of Islam' argues Islam's global spread was driven primarily by trade and pragmatic adaptability rather than coercive power — reaching Guangzhou, China (site of the Huaisheng Mosque) within two decades of the Prophet's death via Indian Ocean and South China Sea merchant routes, and spreading via the same routes to Indonesia roughly 600 years later, now the world's most populous Muslim nation.
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: Undercuts the 'Islam by the sword' framing common in Western political discourse — relevant context for assessing radicalisation narratives against the actual historical drivers of Muslim demographic distribution.
insights.quote: The world that Muslims had made was tied together by worldly interests and material goods, by merchants, money, and trade winds, at least as much as by religious teaching and spiritual values

insights.claim: Up to the 11th century, many Islamic legal scholars either came from merchant families or engaged in trade themselves, reflecting the absence of a separate clergy and Islam's early fusion of commercial success with religious learning.
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: Illustrates a structural feature — merchant-scholar fusion — that shaped Islamic law's practical, commerce-friendly orientation from its early centuries.
insights.quote: many legal scholars up to the eleventh century either came from merchant families, or engaged in trade themselves
insights.locator: Merchants were noted, too, for their displays of piety

insights.claim: President George W. Bush's post-9/11 framing that Bin Laden presented a 'distorted' vision and that 'Islam is peace' is, per Maher, arguably just as mistaken as the harshest critics' view that Islam is inherently violent — both misread Islam's genuinely contested, interpretive nature.
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: Frames both 'Islam is peace' apologetics and 'Islam is inherently violent' polemics as equally reductive — a corrective for anyone using either simplified framing to model geopolitical or terrorism risk.
insights.quote: The face of terror is not the true faith of Islam. That’s not what Islam is all about
insights.locator: President George W. Bush waded into this discussion

insights.claim: Islam has no formal, centralised ordination process or clergy — unlike rabbis or priests, anyone can call themselves 'imam' or 'sheikh' (both originally Arabic honorifics, not credentialed titles) — which makes Islam 'built upon highly democratised foundations,' open to contested interpretation.
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: Explains structurally why Islamic authority is so fragmented and contestable — a key variable for assessing the credibility and durability of any given cleric's or movement's claim to religious legitimacy.
insights.quote: Islam is therefore built upon highly democratised foundations, a religion of contested praxis between those vying to both shape and steer its trajectory.
insights.locator: Much more so than its Abrahamic counterparts

insights.claim: Anjem Choudary marketed himself as 'sheikh' despite having read law at Southampton University and never formally studying Islam, illustrating how the absence of credentialing lets self-styled figures claim religious authority; he was effectively sentenced to life imprisonment in July 2024, and several of his mentor Omar Bakri Mohammed's European acolytes died fighting for ISIS.
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: A concrete case study of how Islam's lack of clerical gatekeeping enabled a specific, consequential radicalisation pipeline in the UK and Europe.
insights.quote: His most famous student, Anjem Choudary, was effectively sentenced to life imprisonment in July 2024.
insights.locator: His most famous student, Anjem Choudary

insights.claim: Islam's self-understanding as humanity's last chance at salvation — a teleological correction to Judaism and Christianity, which it holds became doctrinally diluted and distorted — explains the doctrinal stubbornness of reactionary elements: any compromise risks 'condemning itself to the fate of its predecessors.'
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: A structural, theological explanation (not merely cultural) for why some Islamic factions resist reform — useful for distinguishing genuine doctrinal constraints from purely political resistance when assessing reform prospects in different Muslim-majority states.
insights.quote: Give an inch, and the entire faith risks condemning itself to the fate of its predecessors, which, in turn, negates the entire reason for Islam’s very existence.
insights.locator: This explains the dogged, trenchant stubbornness of reactionaries

insights.claim: Until Mohammed bin Salman's ascension, official Saudi literature routinely described Mohammed ibn Abd al-Wahhab — founder of Wahhabi Islam and the cleric who gave the al-Saud family religious sanction to found the modern Saudi state — as a mujaddid ('renewer'); MBS has since downplayed al-Wahhab's centrality to national folklore.
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: A concrete marker of Saudi Arabia's ongoing religious-legitimacy pivot away from Wahhabism — relevant for tracking the durability and reversibility of MBS's social and economic liberalisation programme.
insights.quote: In the view of the Wahhabis themselves, Ibn Abd al-Wahhab was above all a mujaddid
insights.locator: Until the ascension of Mohammed bin Salman

insights.claim: In a 2021 interview, MBS argued that Ibn Abd al-Wahhab himself would object to being followed blindly and 'deified,' calling for continuous reinterpretation of Quranic and prophetic texts based on 'the time, place, and mindset' — invoking the jurisprudential principle of al-maslaha al-mursalah (acting in the public interest) to open Islamic law to subjective interpretation.
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: MBS's own stated reform doctrine — public-interest jurisprudence — is the explicit legal mechanism underpinning Saudi social liberalisation, a framework for anticipating which further reforms are doctrinally justifiable under his logic.
insights.quote: There are no fixed schools of thought and there is no infallible person. We should engage in continuous interpretation of Quranic texts

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia has moved slower than the UAE on a public programme of religious renewal stressing toleration and moderation, but is 'now racing ahead.'
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: Suggests an inflection point in Saudi religious-liberalisation policy worth monitoring relative to the UAE's earlier head start — relevant for regional soft-power and investment-climate comparisons.
insights.locator: In this respect, Saudi Arabia has been much slower

insights.claim: 12th-century Andalusian mystic Ibn Arabi's concept of wahdat al-wujud ('the unity of existence' — that God is the only reality and all creation merely reflects the divine) provided a historical basis within Islamic mysticism for an 'open-minded view of religious diversity.'
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: Identifies an indigenous Islamic intellectual resource for religious pluralism that reformers can invoke as authentically Islamic rather than Western-imposed — relevant for the credibility of pluralist reform movements.
insights.quote: an open-minded view of religious diversity
insights.locator: Drawing on the work of Ibn Arabi

insights.claim: Indian scholar-politician Abul Kalam Azad adapted wahdat al-wujud into a political argument against the partition of India, framing the division of Hindus and Muslims as an affront to God's unitary design; he was a vocal, prominent opponent of partition.
insights.speaker: Shiraz Maher
insights.so_what: A historical case of Islamic mystical theology deployed for pluralist, anti-sectarian political ends — a counter-example to purely sectarian readings of Islamic politics in South Asia.
insights.quote: The unity of man is the primary aim of religion
insights.locator: This is where the concept of wahdat al-wujud found its greatest expression
